reasoning:
The baseline value for 2026-01-18 (a Sunday) corresponds to the last trading day, Friday, Jan 16, which is 3.60%. This 3.60% value is a local peak, having risen from 3.55% earlier in January following strong jobs and inflation data that caused markets to price out a January rate cut. 

For the first resolution date (2026-01-25), the underlying reference will trace to Friday, Jan 23. The 4-week Treasury bill yield is closely tied to the Effective Federal Funds Rate (EFFR). With the Fed target range securely at 3.50% - 3.75% and no anticipated change at the end-of-January meeting, we expect the 4-week rate to stabilize or slightly mean-revert toward 3.55-3.59% over the next week. Furthermore, the requirement is for a strict increase; since the rate is already at a high plateau for this interval, an increase (>3.60%) is less likely than a flat or slight downward move. I assign a ~35% chance to this.

For the second date (2026-02-17), more time has elapsed. The FOMC hold in January will be priced in. Traders will now look towards the March FOMC meeting, and 4-week bills issued on Feb 17 mature immediately before the mid-March meeting. Downside risk is possible if soft data triggers aggressive pricing of a March cut, but upside risk also exists if data completely eliminates near-term cut chances. Since horizon-based uncertainty increases and potential volatility rises, the probability must regress more toward maximum uncertainty (0.5), as per the prompt rules. I'm assigning it roughly 44%.
